figures.caption: Fig. 1: Example computations of lower and upper bounds on maxmin and min-max values, respectively, in RL1(X,Y ;v) (Theorem 3.1). We showcase a three-contest case study, with contest values shown in the top left diagram. We fix X = 1 and vary Y . The top right plot shows traces for LB∗ (right-hand-side of (9); solid red), UB∗ (right-hand-side of (11); dotted black), and the equilibrium value from the classic GL game, πGL X (dashed blue). We used standard convex solvers to calculate LB∗, and directly applied formulas (11) and (6) to calculate UB∗ and πGL X , respectively. We note a very small gap between LB∗ and UB∗ for values of Y < 1, and they actually coincide for sufficiently high Y ≥ 1, indicating equilibrium solutions. The optimizers α∗ to (9) and p∗ to (11) for six examples are shown in the bottom table. We observe that player Y’s strategy p∗ randomly selects among all three contests when it has a small budget, Y ≤ 0.25. Interestingly, the contest selection probabilities are not proportional to the contest valuations, and it focuses its attack more on the least valuable contest, v3. For higher budgets, player Y abandons the lesser-valued contests in favor of the highest-value contest.
